clinical_history: This ECG, from a young adult male with dyspnea, is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus rhythm at about 92/min, a somewhat high resting rate for a healthy, unstressed adult. The PR and QT/QTc intervals are normal. The QRS duration is at the upper limits of normal as measured by computer (110 ms) with a borderline intraventricular conduction delay (note also the terminal r~ wave in V1). The mean QRS axis is normal at about +60 degrees. Very prominent QRS voltage is present, consistent with left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH), along with extremely large P waves. These are biphasic in V1 and V2 with a broad, wide negative component and are of high amplitude in lead II (>2.5 mm), consistent with biatrial enlargement. R wave progression in V1-V3 is slow, a non-specific finding. Lateral ST-T abnormalities are also non-specific, but are consistent with LVH. The presence of left atrial abnormality and ST-T changes indicate that the prominent precordial voltage cannot be attributed simply to the patient young male or due athletic heart syndrome.  Additional comment: the evidence of biatrial abnormality in concert with signs of LVH and slow R wave progression are suggestive of biventricular hypertrophy/overload. More speculatively, the ~normal~ QRS in this context may represent a ~compromise" between a more leftward axis from LVH and a more rightward axis from RVH.
